coredata.dc:title: Green preparation of carbon dots from plum as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of doxorubicin
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Jiantao
Chu, Huiyuan
Shen, Jiwei
Wang, Chaozhan
Wei, Yinmao
coredata.dc:description: Doxorubicin (DOX) represents an anthracycline-based antitumor and antibiotic medication. The determination of DOX in body fluids is of significance for treatment and individual dosage optimization. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of DOX is established using plum-based carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) prepared form plum as precursors via facile bottom-up method. The prepared PCQDs show green fluorescence with excitation/emission wavelength at 388/491 nm. DOX can be measured by the fluorescence intensity ratio at 591 nm and 491 nm, and the detection concentration range is from 1 to 30 μM with a limit detection of 0.12 μM (3σ/k). The method shows good anti-interference and is successfully applied to the determination of DOX in human urine and human serum samples with satisfactory recoveries (92.61–116.64%), indicating that this method can be used as an alternative in the detection of DOX in clinic.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Doxorubicin
Plum-based carbon quantum dots
Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Detection

Characterization of plum-based carbon quantum dots
Optical properties of plum-based carbon quantum dots
Mechanism of the detection of DOX
Analysis of DOX in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (a), particle size distribution histogram (b), XRD patterns (c) and FT-IR spectra (d) of PCQDs.
Absorbance, excitation and emission spectra of PCQDs (a). Fluorescence emission spectra of PCQDs with different excitation wavelengths in the range of 328–418 nm (b).
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of PCQDs with the addition of DOX. (b) Ratio of fluorescence intensities at 491 nm and 591 nm (I591/I491) as a function of DOX concentration.

I591/I491 for the mixture of PCQDs and the selected species (0: blank, 1: 1 mmol K+; 2: 500 mmol Na+, 3: 200 μM Fe3+, 4: 500 μM Cr3+, 5: 500 μM Mg2+, 6: 500 μM Ca2+, 7: 500 μM Co2+, 8: 500 μM Al3+, 9: 500 μM Cu2+, 10: 500 μM Zn2+, 11: 500 μM SO4 2−, 12: 500 mmol Cl−,13: 500 μM glycine, 14: 500 μM glutamic acid, 15: 500 μM proline, 16: 500 μM threonine, 17: 500 μM valine, 18: 500 μM histidine, 19: 500 μM uric acid, 20: 500 μM dopamine, 21: 500 μM urea, 22: 500 μM glutathione, 23: 500 μM glucose, 24: 500 μM ascorbic acid, 25: 25 μM DOX, 26: 250 μM 5-methyluridine, 27: 250 μM adenosine, 28: 2 mg/mL human serum albumin, 29: 250 μM cytidine, 30: 250 μM guanosine, 31: 250 μM thymidine.
Scheme of the preparation of PCQDs and the detection of DOX.
The determined recoveries of DOX in real samples by the fluorescence probe.

head.ce:title: Green preparation of carbon dots from plum as a ratiometric fluorescent probe for detection of doxorubicin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Synthetic and Natural Functional Molecule Chemistry of Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Northwest University, Xi'an, 710127, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Doxorubicin (DOX) represents an anthracycline-based antitumor and antibiotic medication. The determination of DOX in body fluids is of significance for treatment and individual dosage optimization. In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the detection of DOX is established using plum-based carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) prepared form plum as precursors via facile bottom-up method. The prepared PCQDs show green fluorescence with excitation/emission wavelength at 388/491 nm. DOX can be measured by the fluorescence intensity ratio at 591 nm and 491 nm, and the detection concentration range is from 1 to 30 μM with a limit detection of 0.12 μM (3σ/k). The method shows good anti-interference and is successfully applied to the determination of DOX in human urine and human serum samples with satisfactory recoveries (92.61–116.64%), indicating that this method can be used as an alternative in the detection of DOX in clinic.

Preparation and ratiometric fluorescence schematic of plum-based carbon quantum dots (PCQDs). The sensor shows better anti-interference over the reported methods and can be employed in the detection of DOX in human urine and serum.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Doxorubicin (DOX), a broad anti-tumor antibiotic, is widely used for the treatment of various cancers. It can kill cancer cells by inhibiting the synthesis of RNA and DNA in the cancer cell cycle [1,2]. On the other hand, DOX has some side-effects of causing bone marrow suppression and cardiotoxicity [3]. In clinic, monitoring the concentration of DOX in human biofluids can guide its reasonable clinical dosage, thereby ensuring the effectiveness of treatment and minimizing the toxicity to patients. At present, there are many techniques for the determination of DOX, including high performance liquid chromatography [4,5], microchip capillary electrophoresis [6], voltammetric [7], chemiluminescence [8]. In comparison, fluorescence assays have received more attention due to high selectivity, sensitivity, fast analysis and simplicity [9–12]. In fluorescence assays, the ratiometric fluorescence assay quantitatively detects the target by calculating the ratio of two signals measured at the same excitation wavelength, which shows many advantages over single-wavelength fluorescence assay in minimizing the impacts of instruments, background and environmental signals [13,14]. As far as the detection of DOX is concerned, most of the developed fluorescence methods were single-wavelength measurement [9–12]. To date, Xu et al. reported the only ratio fluorescence probe in the detection of DOX [15]. In the work, the preparation process needs four steps: preparation of aminopropyl silica and carbon quantum dots, modification of carbon quantum dots onto silica and surface imprinting, being rather complex to complete. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple ratio fluorescent probe for the detection of DOX.

As a novel fluorescence material, carbon quantum dots have been investigated extensively due to good water solubility, biocompatibility, optical stability and low toxicity [16]. There are several preparation methods of carbon dots, including chemical oxidation, electrochemical oxidation processes, carbonizing organics routes, hydrothermal and microwave irradiation [17]. Nevertheless, these ways were costly and required toxic chemicals along with multi-step synthesis. In the contrary, hydrothermal is simple and economical, especially natural biomass as a precursor is attractive. The biomass-derived carbon quantum dots from natural plants have been employed in for the detection of drugs, ions, biomolecular substances [18]. However, the fluorescence sensor based on biomass-derived carbon quantum dots has not been reported for the detection of DOX. In terms of simple, economical and green availability, the development of a new probe using biomass-derived carbon quantum dots is of value for the detection of DOX.

Based on these considerations, we prepared the plum-based carbon quantum dots (PCQDs) via a one-step solvothermal method by using plum as precursors. The fluorescence emission wavelength of PCQDs is in the range of green light and is variable to excitation wavelength. Particularly, PCQDs can be directly applied as a ratiometric fluorescence probe of DOX based on inner filter effect (IFE) (Scheme 1 ). The probe shows the advantages of sensitivity, selectivity and anti-interference in the detection of DOX in human urine and serum.

Plum was purchased from local markets in Xi'an, China. DOX (98%), cytarabine (98%), Cytoxan (98%), 5-fluorouracil (99%) and methotrexate (99%) were purchased from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China, http://macklin.company.lookchem.cn). Other regents were analytical reagent grade and all of the reagents are used directly without any purification. Water was obtained from Millipore water system.

The morphology and size were observed on a transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI Talos F200X, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a TENSOR27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out with PHI5000 VersaProbe III (ULVAC-PHI, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were performed through X-ray Powder diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). The fluorescence spectra were measured on a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301PC, Shimadzu, Japan). Absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were performed on a photon-counting spectrometer (FLSP920, Edinburgh Instruments, England) with λex = 370 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of plum-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: 10 g plums were washed with tap water and cut into small pieces. Then, the small pieces were extracted four times with 40 mL of ethanol and the solution was filtered through 0.22 μm membrane. The solvent was removed by rotary evaporation and the residues were redistributed into 10 mL of ethanol. Finally, the mixture was transferred to a 15 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The dark brown solution was centrifuged at 13,200 g for 20 min, filtered through 0.22 μm and evaporated to obtain a black solid.

The quantum yield (QY) of PCQDs (in water) was measured with quinine sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4 as a solvent; QY = 0.54) as a standard. Absolute values were calculated according to the following equation:
(1)
Q Y =
( Q
Y S
)
(
I X
I S
)
(
η X 2
η S 2
)
(
A S
A X
)
Where QY represents the quantum yield of PCQDs, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, η is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the absorption density. The subscript ‘‘s’’ refers to parameters of compound and ‘‘x’’ refers to the unknown samples. In this paper, QY of PCQDs was calculated using Quinone sulfate (0.1 M H2SO4) which has known quantum yield 0.54 at excitation wavelength 360 nm.

In a typical experiment, 100 μL of DOX solution with different concentrations was mixed with 200 μL 1.0 mg/mL PCQDs solution; the mixture was diluted to 2 mL with phosphate buffer solution (10 mM, pH 7.0). The fluorescence spectra of the mixture were measured with the excitation wavelength at 388 nm. The slit widths of excitation and emission were set at 5 and 10 nm, respectively. The fluorescence signal (I591/I491) of the mixture was recorded to evaluate the concentration of DOX.

The human urine and plasma samples from healthy adult males in our group were collected under the guidelines of the Ethics Committee of the Institute, Northwest University. The urine was centrifuged at 10744 g for 20 min and filtered through 0.22 μm membrane filter. The plasma was centrifuged at 4298 g for 5 min to obtain a serum. Then, the blank urine and serum were spiked with known concentration of DOX (3, 10, 20 μM), respectively. Finally, the standard curve method was used to determine DOX in urine and serum samples. The concentrations of DOX were the average of three parallel trials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of plum-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: As well known, the extract of plum contains gibberellin A/32, β-carcotene, cryptoxanthin, lutein, sugars, amino acids, and minerals. During the thermal process, the natural carbonaceous compounds passed through the polymerization and sequential carbonization processes to produce the PCQDs. As confirmed by TEM image (Fig. 1 a), the morphology of PCQDs have good dispersity with an average size of 1.8 nm (Fig. 1b). In the XRD spectra, there is a broad peak at 2θ = 210 (Fig. 1c) corresponding to the (002) plane of graphite, indicating the presence of graphitic carbon domain in the PCQDs [19]. To gain insights into the structural information of PCQDs, XPS and FT-IR were carried out, respectively. In FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1d), there are the characteristic absorption peaks at 3445, 2923, 1650, 1378, and 1081 cm−1, which are attributed to the vibrational absorption peaks of O–H/N–H, C–H, CO, C–N, and C–O [20,21], respectively. XPS analysis shows that the PCQDs are mainly composed of C, N, and O (Fig. 2 a) and the relative element percentages of C, N and O are 86.29 wt%, 6.51 wt% and 7.20 wt%, respectively. In the high-resolution C 1s, N 1s and O1s spectra (Fig. 2b–d), there are C–C (284.6 eV), C–O/C–N (286.7 eV), CO/CN (288.2 eV) [22], graphite-like N (396.9 eV), pyridinic-like N (398.1 eV), pyrrodic-like N (399.5 eV) [23], C–O (530.7 eV) and –COOH (532.6 eV) bonds [24]. Since most of the groups are polar groups, PCQDs have good compatibility with aqueous solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of plum-based carbon quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the optical properties of the PCQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of PCQDs have two characteristic absorption bands (Fig. 3a), which could be assigned to π-π* transition of sp2 domains (230 nm) and n-π* transition (280 nm) [25]. Under the excitation at 388 nm, the obtained fluorescence spectra of PCQDs exhibit maximum fluorescence intensity at 491 nm. Also, the fluorescence emission spectra of PCQDs were recorded at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths (Fig. 3b). Upon increasing the excitation wavelength from 328 to 418 nm, the emission peak gradually shifts to long wavelength. The phenomenon was consistent with the optical properties of biomass-based carbon dots reported by Vaibhavkumar [26,27], and related to the excitation-dependent fluorescence property of carbon dots. The reason was ascribed to the size distribution and different energy levels associated with various surface states [28,29]. The QY of PCQDs was calculated to be 2.84% when quinine in 0.1 M H2SO4 was used as reference, which was close to the value of many biomass-carbon dots summarized by Liu [18].

The stability of fluorescence materials plays a key role in sensitive fluorescence detection. As shown above, PCQDs have abundant amino and carboxyl groups. Since the dissociation extents of these groups depend upon pH, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs was investigated (Fig. S1a). The fluorescence intensity of PCQDs shows a mildly increasing trend against the variation of pH from 2.0 to 8.0 and significantly decreases at pH > 8.0. The main reason could be ascribed to the reversible protonation/de-protonation of PCQDs in acidic and alkaline media [30]. These results illustrate that the stability and tolerability of PCQDs are from pH 2.0 to 8.0, enabling a wide range of applications. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs remains almost constant upon continuous illumination with the 150 W Xe lamp for 1h, showing the good photostability and negligible photobleaching of PCQDs (Fig. S1b). The effect of ionic strength was studied by treating PCQDs with different concentrations of NaCl (0.0–1.0 M). The fluorescence intensity of PCQDs decreases only 5.7% upon increasing concentrations of NaCl from 0.0 to 1.0 M (Fig. S1c). Moreover, as temperature rises to 50 °C, the fluorescence intensity mildly decreases only 8.9% (Fig. S1d). Such fluorescence stability implies that PCQDs as fluorescence probe have potential in practical application.

To evaluate the application of PCQDs in analytical chemistry, PCQDs were used as a sensor for the detection of DOX. Firstly, fluorescence parameters including sample pH and incubation time were optimized. Considering the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs and I591/I491, pH 7.0 was selected as the experiment condition in the further study (Fig. S2). It was found that incubation time of 1 min can give the best fluorescence quenching efficiency (Fig. S3). Under the optimal conditions, there is a dependence of the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs on the concentration of DOX (Fig. 4 a). Upon increasing concentrations of DOX, the fluorescence intensity at 491 nm goes down gradually whereas the fluorescence intensity at 591 nm assigned to DOX increases simultaneously. The ratio of the two fluorescence intensities (I591/I491) is linearly proportional to the DOX concentration in the range of 1–30 μM and 30–70 μM with a good linear correlation (R2 ≥ 0.994), respectively (Fig. 4b). The limit detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.12 μM based on 3σ/k (where σ is the standard deviation of the blank, and k is the slope of the linear calibration plot), which is suitable to detect the residual DOX concentration in the cancer patients (7.37–15.67 μM) [31].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of the detection of DOX
ce:section.ce:para: Generally, the mechanisms of fluorescence sensor include photoinduced electron transfer, metal-ligand charge transfer, electronic energy transfer, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and IFE [32]. As seen in Fig. 4a, the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs can be efficiently quenched by DOX in solution. The possible quenching mechanism was further investigated. Fig. S4 is the UV–vis spectra of DOX and the excitation and emission spectra of PCQDs. It can be seen that the DOX presents a broad absorption spectrum from 400 nm to 600 nm with a maximum absorption at 490 nm, which shows quite precise overlapping with the emission bands of PCQDs. This phenomenon can cause the FRET or IFE between PCQDs and DOX [33]. The FRET belongs to the dynamic quenching mechanism because energy transfer just occurs in the excited-stated and is expected to substantially change the life of fluorophore [34,35]. In order to confirm the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetimes of PCQDs in the absence and presence of 10 μM and 15 μM DOX were determined (Fig. S5). The lifetimes of mixture are almost the same as that of PCQDs, indicating no energy transfer process between PCQDs and DOX [19]. Therefore, all these results demonstrate that the observed fluorescence quenching is aroused from the IFE of DOX on the fluorescence of PCQDs.

Since DOX has certain fluorescence characteristics, it has been directly employed for fluorescence analysis of DOX [36]. To indicate the advantage of our method, the direct fluorescence method was conducted as a control. As shown in Fig. S8 a and b, the fluorescence intensity at 591 nm (I591) is linearly proportional to DOX concentration in the range of 0.25–3.5 μM. The LOD was calculated to be 0.029 μM based on 3σ/k. In addition, Cu2+ among the tested interferents has a strong fluorescence quenching toward DOX, which affects the determination of DOX in the direct fluorescence method (Fig. S8c). However, Cu2+ has no effect on I591/I491 (Fig. S8d), indicating better anti-interference performance of the ratio fluorescence probe in this work. In addition, the ratio fluorescence probe has a comparable LOD and a wider linear range than the direct fluorescence method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of DOX in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of the ratiometric fluorescence probe, the analytical method in this work was used to detect DOX in urine and serum. The urine and serum samples were taken from healthy adult men, so they contained no target analyte. Under optimized conditions, the blank samples were spiked with standard solutions containing different concentrations of DOX, and the fluorescence spectra were recorded. As shown in Figs. S9 and S10, the fluorescence intensity of PCQDs decreases with increasing concentration of DOX from 1 μM to 30 μM, and I591/I491 has a good linear relationship with concentration of DOX in the range of 1–30 μM (R2 > 0.978). In addition, the accuracy and precision of the method were investigated through the recovery test. The concentrations of DOX in plasma of cancerous patients vary widely depending on the dose used and the drug administration regimen prescribed, but normally the reported concentration range is 7.37–15.67 μM according to the Physician's Desk Reference [31]. According to the concentration range in clinic, the spiked high, medium and low concentrations of DOX were thus set to 20 μM, 10 μM and 3 μM, respectively, covering the concentration of DOX in real samples. It can be seen from Table 1 that the recoveries for the DOX are in the range of 92.61–116.64%, the intra-day RSDs are less than 2.45% and the inter-day RSDs are less than 4.56%, indicating the good accuracy and precision of this method. Therefore, the established method can be used in the detection of DOX in clinic samples.

In general, the PCQDs have been prepared via a facile bottom-up method using plum as precursor. The PCQDs show green fluorescence, good photostability and solubility, and can be applied for sensitive and ratio determination of DOX based on the IFE process between the PCQDs and DOX. In analysis of DOX, the ratio fluorescence probe has good anti-interference and a low LOD that covers the concentration range of DOX in cancer patients. In addition, the probe was used to detect the level of DOX in urine and serum with satisfactory results. For the advantage of simple preparation and favourable anti-interference, the PCQDs have great potential application for the DOX detection in clinic.

Jiantao Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Investigation. Huiyuan Chu: Software, Validation. Jiwei Shen: Writing – review & editing. Chaozhan Wang: Resources. Yinmao Wei: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21775121 and 21974106).
